The precipitable water value was a between 1.5 and 1.8 inches.  The LCL was relatively low, around 1000 meters.  Thunderstorms moved into western Iowa out of Nebraska. As the afternoon and evening progressed, the thunderstorm complex evolved into one that was more outflow dominated.  The system raced southeast and produced high winds across the Des Moines metro area and to the east and south.  Winds of 60 to 80 MPH caused considerable tree and power line damage.  Power outages were reported by 27,000 customers in Des Moines at the height of the storm.  As the storms moved through the heart of Des Moines, a homeless individual died when a large tree branch fell on him at a homeless settlement along the banks of the Raccoon River east of Fluer Blvd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree about 8 inches in diameter was snapped.

An upper level storm system moved across the high Plains during the afternoon on Tuesday June 3rd, and then across the mid Missouri River Valley on Tuesday night. Ahead of the system, warm, moist and unstable air was pulled north as a warm front lifted into eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa by mid afternoon. The combination of the strong vertical wind shear near the boundary, and the strong instability created a volatile atmosphere across the area. The first of several rounds of supercell thunderstorms moved into northeast Nebraska after 1 pm and tracked east southeast. This intense storm moved across northeast Nebraska, and into western and finally southern Iowa producing severe weather. Additional thunderstorms developed ahead and behind this initial wave, also producing severe weather. Additional supercells developed in central Nebraska and moved into southeast Nebraska during the evening hours. The severe weather ended after midnight across the area. Due to the repeated tracking of thunderstorms across the same area, flash flooding was observed in several areas which eventually led to river flooding as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported that several large trees were downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms moved northeastward across parts of eastern Iowa, northeast Missouri, and western Illinois between 3 and 6 pm CDT May 8 as a cold front approached the area from the west.  Some of the storms were severe, producing damaging wind gusts and isolated quarter sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated to be 50 to 60 mph were observed about 4 miles north northeast of Vinton, IA at 525 pm CDT May 8.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms developed late in the afternoon of June 16th across Minnesota and Iowa ahead of warm front and area of low pressure. These storms produced reports of 60 to 70 mph winds as they rolled across northeast Iowa. The area near Westgate (Fayette County) was hit hard with at least three barns blown down, a church roof damaged and numerous trees and power lines were downed. Another cattle barn was blown down south of Brainard (Fayette County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least three trees were blown down on the southeast side of Riceville.

Low pressure developed over southwest Kansas during the night of the 19th into the day of the 20th.  The low turned northeast through Missouri into east central Iowa by the afternoon of the 20th.  A strong surge of moisture pushed north ahead of the low with dewpoint temperatures in the 40s to low 50s.  Strong dynamics were in place with around 500 J/kg of CAPE available.  A small area of thunderstorms pushed rapidly northeast through the state producing gusty winds and hail as they passed.  Hail covered the ground in many locations.  Though marginal in size, one inch diameter hail was reported in Union County at Arispe.  The line of storms also produced hail up to quarter size as they moved through Warren and Wayne Counties.  As the line of severe thunderstorms moved through Ringgold County, very strong winds occurred.  The winds resulted in an accident to an individual working outdoors near farming equipment. While attempting to move his pickup truck to get it out of hail that was falling, a 30' conveyor belt was blown over by a wind gust, falling on him and causing his death.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An adult male died when a 30 foot conveyor belt was blown over by a wind gust.  The incident occurred at 2401 Co. Hwy P-27 at the Helena Chemical Company.

Behind this system, across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa strong northwest developed during the morning and peaked in intensity during the early afternoon. Many locations across northeast Nebraska and west central Iowa recorded wind gusts greater than 60 mph with sustained winds greater than 40 mph during this time. The strong winds created some minor damage, power outages and blowing dust. Winds diminished during the evening hours on the 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful storm system lifted into the central Plains on the afternoon of  April 27, 2014 and brought several rounds of showers and thunderstorms to eastern Iowa. Some of these thunderstorms were severe and produced damaging winds and an isolated hail report. There was one confirmed tornado during the event that moved across Keokuk, Iowa and Johnson Counties with two fatalities. In addition to the damaging winds rainfall totals ranged from a half an inch to around 1 inch in a little under an hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Benton County Emergency Manager reported a building blown down along with power lines. a tractor trailer was jack-knifed on Highway 30.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms in northwest Iowa during the late afternoon and evening of June 14th produced heavy rain which caused flash flooding. The thunderstorms also produced damaging winds and hail. The heavy rain also caused longer term lowland flooding of fields and other low areas including roads, that lasted well into the daytime hours of June 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced numerous events of large hail, damaging winds, flash flooding, and areal flooding across most of northwest Iowa from late morning through the evening of June 16th. There was also one brief tornado. The areal flooding was a longer term event and persisted through much of June 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm airmass moved into Iowa on the 11th and 12th of April.  By the afternoon of the 12th, temperatures reached the 80s across a large part of Iowa with dewpoint temperatures in the mid 50s to around 60.  A cold front moved into Iowa during the afternoon and tracked southeast across the state during the evening and overnight hours.  The atmosphere became unstable with CAPE values increasing to 2000 to 2500 J/kg by late afternoon.   Downdraft CAPE rose to between 800 and 1200 J/kg, while CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was in the 400 to 500 J/kg range  The freezing level was around 12,000 ft near the storms inception point.  Precipitable water values increased to just over an inch by evening.  The atmosphere was strongly sheared with 40 to 60 kts of effective shear available.  Thunderstorms were initially capped off during much of the afternoon with only scattered storms forming along the front. The cap was broken by late afternoon into the evening. A line of strong to severe storms formed.  The primary mode of severe weather was hail with most storms producing at least some hail.  There were numerous reports of golf ball size hail.  A few of the storms produced larger hail, with 2.5 inch diameter hail reported in Story County, and 2 inch diameter hail in Butler and Bremer Counties.  After sunset, the storms began to produce severe winds with several gusts to around 60 MPH reported over the southeast third of the state.  As the winds increased, the hail became smaller.  Significant damage was done in Mahaska County by the strong winds.  Heavy straight-line winds caused several thousand dollars in damage to a farm.  The barn roof was damaged, a south wall partially gone, overhead door destroyed and wrapped around a trailer, skylights blown out, north wall section bowed out, and a 12' X 14' door was blown off. Three fruit trees were also blown down.  Roof damage was reported in Wayne County as very strong straight-line winds swept through the Promise City area.  In addition, a large shed was blown down by the winds south of Corydon.  During the night into the next morning the storms became multi-cellular and began to train over the state as the next shortwave advanced northeast toward the central U.S.  Locally heavy rain was reported with a 3.5 inch total in a 6-hour period reported in Clarke County of south central Iowa.  Twenty-four hour rainfall totals in Jasper and Poweshiek Counties were around 4 inches.  Two-day totals across much of the southeast half of Iowa were in the two to five inch range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large shed was destroyed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A seasonably strong shortwave trough moved into the northern Plains during the day on Monday July 7th. This allowed for a cold front to advance southeast across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa during the late afternoon and evening hours. Due to persistent clouds, as well as frontal passage the previous day resulting in limited moisture, the atmosphere was only moderately unstable for July standards. A band of elevated showers and thunderstorms slowly intensified as they moved southeast across the area. The thunderstorms become the most intense over far southwest Iowa and southeast Nebraska closer to a stalled frontal boundary. The thunderstorms quickly moved into northeast Kansas and northwest Missouri by late evening ending the severe weather threat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea size hail was also reported from the spotter in addition to the strong winds.

A strong cold front pushed into a very warm unstable air mass as a significant upper level storm system tracked into the northern Great Lakes on June 29-30, 2014. A major severe weather and flash flood event unfolded across the area. This event resulted in the direct deaths of 2 people and direct injuries to 7 others.  Three weak tornadoes, widespread damaging winds of 60 to 80 mph, and life-threatening flash flooding due to 4 to 6 inches of rainfall was reported. Two of the tornadoes were in northeast Iowa while another tornado was spotted in northwest Illinois.  Law enforcement reported a business building destroyed by high winds from a severe thunderstorm in Fairfax, Iowa. One person was killed and another injured due to the collapsed structure.  A severe thunderstorm packing 80 mph winds blew a tarp on top of several people trying to cover the infield at Veterans Memorial Stadium in Cedar Rapids, Iowa. Three people suffered concussions while another person had a dislocated shoulder.  In North Liberty, Iowa a tree fell on a person who was then transported to a local hospital.  Severe flash flooding in Cedar Rapids, Iowa resulted in the drowning death of a teenage boy with another boy injured.  They were swept into a storm sewer and were found a mile away in a lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The emergency manager reported numerous large trees down, some on homes, and power lines also down.  The damage occurred mainly in the central portion of the city of Clinton.  The winds were estimated at 70 to 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of thunderstorms formed over northeast Nebraska in an areas of strong theta-e advection.  Precipitable water values were only around 1.3 inches.  CAPE was in the 1000 to 2000 J/kg range with a downdraft CAPE of 800 J/kg.  The atmosphere was strongly sheared with about 50 kts of effective shear present.  The area of storms raced east at 55 to 65 MPH.  The storm produced high winds, as well as tree and power line damage, as it moved through Kossuth, Humboldt, and Wright Counties.  Wright County was hardest hit with some structural damage done to homes west of Clarion.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A four foot tree was down on a house and car in the 1000 block of S. Main St.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed into a very warm unstable air mass as a significant upper level storm system tracked into the northern Great Lakes on June 29-30, 2014. A major severe weather and flash flood event unfolded across the area. This event resulted in the direct deaths of 2 people and direct injuries to 7 others.  Three weak tornadoes, widespread damaging winds of 60 to 80 mph, and life-threatening flash flooding due to 4 to 6 inches of rainfall was reported. Two of the tornadoes were in northeast Iowa while another tornado was spotted in northwest Illinois.  Law enforcement reported a business building destroyed by high winds from a severe thunderstorm in Fairfax, Iowa. One person was killed and another injured due to the collapsed structure.  A severe thunderstorm packing 80 mph winds blew a tarp on top of several people trying to cover the infield at Veterans Memorial Stadium in Cedar Rapids, Iowa. Three people suffered concussions while another person had a dislocated shoulder.  In North Liberty, Iowa a tree fell on a person who was then transported to a local hospital.  Severe flash flooding in Cedar Rapids, Iowa resulted in the drowning death of a teenage boy with another boy injured.  They were swept into a storm sewer and were found a mile away in a lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power line down across the city of Cedar Rapids.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front extended from eastern Nebraska to western Illinois and was lifting northward on June 16-17, 2014.  An extremely unstable air mass was over the area on June 16 along with strong deep layer shear.  This allowed scattered supercells to form during the afternoon and evening which produced two tornadoes, very large hail and damaging winds.  A weak tornado formed 4 miles south of Lamont, Iowa in Buchanan County, tracked about 1 mile to the northeast before crossing into Delaware County. In Delaware County the tornado continued for about 13 miles to the northeast to Edgewood, Iowa.  Another weak tornado briefly touched down in Benton County, Iowa. Strengthening mid level winds along with a strong low level jet in place later in the evening and overnight on June 17 produced widespread damaging winds of 60 to 70 mph.  Strong warm air advection triggered more severe thunderstorms during the late evening of June 17.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported several 5 to 6 inch diameter tree limbs down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell thunderstorms that developed along the intersection of a warm front and dry-line/cold front in eastern Nebraska moved into western Iowa during the evening of May 11th.  These storms spawned tornadoes in Nebraska, but in western Iowa they mainly dropped large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree damage, which knocked down some power lines, was mainly reported on the northwest side of Council Bluffs. At the peak there were 3168 customers around Council Bluffs without power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved southeast across the state during the day on the 26th.  As the low passed to the southeast of the area a strong Arctic cold front moved south across the state.  Temperatures fell from the 40s and 50s into the single digits within a matter of a few hours  Strong northwest winds occurred in the wake of this cold front, along with the sharply falling temperatures.  Sustained winds were in the 30 to 45 MPH range for several hours, with wind gusts to near 65 MPH.  Travel became difficult with reports of some cars and trucks being swept off of the road due to the high cross winds.

A strong low moved from northern Alberta southeast to Lake Superior during the night of the 15th through the day on the 16th. A strong upper level trough was associated with the low. The pressure gradient was strong with winds at the 850 mb level around 50 kts. During the afternoon a pool of very cold air at 850 mb settled into Iowa with temperatures at the core of -24 C. The combination of the atmosphere becoming super-adiabatic during the day and the tightening gradient resulted in strong winds across the state. Winds of 25 to 45 MPH were common with wind gusts over 50 MPH across most of the state. Western Iowa had the highest winds with winds exceeding 58 MPH at several locations. Winds of 61 MPH were reported in Carroll County at Carroll. Atlantic reported a 59 MPH wind gust in Cass County, and a 58 MPH gust was reported in Union County at Creston. Many areas of western Iowa reported sustained winds of 40 MPH. East of the high wind area, a vorticity maximum moved south across central into southeast Iowa. This produced a snow burst with the circulation evident on radar. Thunder accompanied the snow in central Iowa with snowfall of 1 to 3 inches . Visibility was reduced to near zero in heavy snow and blowing snow especially in rural open areas. Visibility increased rapidly as the snow ended as there was no snow on the ground initially, thus blowing snow diminished. Temperatures dropped quickly from the mid 30s into the teens as the snow moved in, allowing for a layer of ice to develop rapidly on roadways. Much of the event hit during the evening commute with several accidents reported with travel becoming hazardous due to the icy conditions and reduced visibilities.

A strong low pressure system moved southeast across Minnesota, reaching southeast Minnesota by sunrise, and continued east into southern Wisconsin by the afternoon.  A strong pressure rise/fall couplet was associated with the low as the mixed layer exhibited a 40 to 50 kt northwest flow.  Strong winds spread across the state in the morning.  Most locations were just below high wind criteria with sustained winds around 35 mph with gusts of 45 to 55 MPH.  A few locations had high wind gusts.  The RWIS site near Mason City reported a wind gust of 64 MPH around 0700 CST.  Just before noon, the RWIS site in Carroll County near Carroll reported at 58 MPH wind gust.

A very dynamic weather pattern was in place over over Iowa.  A warm front lifted north into the state.  Temperatures warmed in the 80s with dew point readings in the upper 60s to mid 70s.  The atmosphere became very unstable with 3000 to 4000 J/kg of CAPE and downdraft CAPE of 1000 to 1500 J/kg.  The shear was strong with 50 to 60 kts available.  The freezing level was quite high, between 13,000 and 14,000 feet wtih CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere between 700 and 900 J/kg.  The precipitable water value was a between 1.5 and 1.8 inches.  The LCL was relatively low, around 1000 meters.  Thunderstorms moved into western Iowa out of Nebraska. As the afternoon and evening progressed, the thunderstorm complex evolved into one that was more outflow dominated.  The system raced southeast and produced high winds across the Des Moines metro area and to the east and south.  Winds of 60 to 80 MPH caused considerable tree and power line damage.  Power outages were reported by 27,000 customers in Des Moines at the height of the storm.  As the storms moved through the heart of Des Moines, a homeless individual died when a large tree branch fell on him at a homeless settlement along the banks of the Raccoon River east of Fluer Blvd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Swath of RFD winds south of the two tornadoes. Large tree snapped on farmstead. Empty grain bin rolled 75 yards to the ENE. Many tree limbs downed for a mile north and several miles E and W of this point.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Area of wind damage downstream of the dissipating tornado east of Clarksville. Limb damage to over a dozen trees and an outbuilding with a west facing entrance was pulled from the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very dynamic pattern was in place over the state with a closed low located over southern Canada into the north central U.S.  A very strong jet of 85 to 100 kts as present at 250 mb.  CAPE by early morning was already 3000 to 4000 J/kg with downdraft CAPE of 1000 to 1400 J/kg.  CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was between 1000 and 1400 J/kg.  The freezing level was around 14,000 feet with precipitable water values near 2 inches.  The LCL remained around 750 meters with 75 J/kg of CAPE in the 0-3 km layer.  There was strong shear present, with 60-80 kts of effective shear.  A cold front moved into Iowa during the day and moved across the state.  This event was similar to the event of 29 June 1998.  Thunderstorms formed over northeast Nebraska by sunrise and continued to propagate east into Iowa.  The storms intensified as they moved across the state, into a forward moving MCS.  As it strengthened into a Derecho, winds of 70 to 80 MPH were reported over a large swath of the state.  All modes of severe weather occurred.  Widespread wind damage was reported to trees, power lines, and structures from the high winds.   In addition, very large hail occurred.  Some of the larger hail included three to four inch diameter hail in Calhoun County near Rockwell City, and three and one half inch diameter hail in Warren County at Sandyville.  Several tornadoes were confirmed.    Very heavy rainfall occurred with the storms.  The rain lead to flooding and flash flooding in many areas of northern and central Iowa.  By the end of the month, a presidential disaster declaration had been made for the follow 9 Iowa counties: Adams, Clarke, Decatur, Mills, Montgomery, Pottawattamie, Ringgold, Taylor and Wayne.  Governor Branstad also requested a disaster declaration for the following 26 Iowa Counties: Allamakee, Buchanan, Buena Vista, Butler, Cherokee, Chickasaw, Clay, Dickinson, Emmet, Fayette, Franklin, Hancock, Humboldt, Ida, Kossuth, Lyon, Osceola, Palo Alto, Plymouth, Pocahontas, Sac, Sioux, Winnebago, Winneshiek, Woodbury, and Wright.  The Presidential declaration was granted on 24 July 2014.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees snapped with limbs across Highway 69.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed during the evening of June 29th ahead of a cold front. As these storms moved across northeast Iowa they produced large hail and damaging winds. Two pole sheds were destroyed near Boyd (Chickasaw County) where baseball sized hail also fell. The large hail destroyed crops in an area from Nashua northeast toward Boyd. In Calmar (Winneshiek County), several trees were blown down and golf ball sized hail damaged several vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two pole sheds were destroyed and there was sporadic crop damage just north of Boyd.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down in New Hampton. One of the trees landed on a house causing minimal damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A seasonably strong upper level storm system swept across the northern Plains during the day on Sunday August 31st. Ahead of the storm system low pressure developed over the high Plains and allowed for warm and moist air to pool over the mid Missouri River Valley creating strong instability by mid to late afternoon. As a cold front and associated upper level jet streak interacted with the instability, thunderstorms developed from northeast Nebraska into Northern Kansas. These thunderstorms moved east through the rest of the afternoon and evening hours, crossing eastern Nebraska and western Iowa produced mainly hail and wind damage, but heavy rainfall also occurred in parts of the area as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported wind gusts to 60 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Swath of wind damage that began in far NE Wright County broadened and strengthened as it entered NW Franklin County. An EF1 tornado was embedded within this wind damage. Wind gusts of 70-80 mph were found in a 1 to 2 mile wide and 4 to 5 mile long corridor, with locally higher gusts over 100 mph. One house lost half of its roof and multiple outbuildings were destroyed. One hog confinement building disintegrated--its remnants scattered for over 2 miles to the south. Damage point is at the house that lost its roof. Hog building was 1.5 miles SE of this point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm system moved across the high Plains during the afternoon on Tuesday June 3rd, and then across the mid Missouri River Valley on Tuesday night. Ahead of the system, warm, moist and unstable air was pulled north as a warm front lifted into eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa by mid afternoon. The combination of the strong vertical wind shear near the boundary, and the strong instability created a volatile atmosphere across the area. The first of several rounds of supercell thunderstorms moved into northeast Nebraska after 1 pm and tracked east southeast. This intense storm moved across northeast Nebraska, and into western and finally southern Iowa producing severe weather. Additional thunderstorms developed ahead and behind this initial wave, also producing severe weather. Additional supercells developed in central Nebraska and moved into southeast Nebraska during the evening hours. The severe weather ended after midnight across the area. Due to the repeated tracking of thunderstorms across the same area, flash flooding was observed in several areas which eventually led to river flooding as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured at the Council Bluffs Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture moved into Iowa as a strong shortwave moved into the central U.S.  Moisture from former hurricane Norbert moved into the central U.S. as a low pressure system developed over Kansas.  The low from Kansas moved northeast into Iowa during the night of the 9th and morning of the 10th.  The low was over southwest Wisconsin on the morning of the 10th.  The atmosphere was unstable with 1000 to 2000 J/kg of CAPE available.  CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was in the 300 to 600 J/kg range with downdraft CAPE around 500 J/kg.  Strong shear was present with 45 to 65 kts of effective shear.  The LCL was fairly low, between 500 and 750 meters.  A deep cloud depth was present with the freezing level around 14,000 feet.  Precipitable water values increased through the afternoon and evening of the 9th and were greater than 2 inches over a large part of the state.  Very little severe weather occurred with the system.  A severe cell moved across southwest Iowa and produced 1 to 2 inch diameter hail in Adams and Taylor Counties.  Severe winds were also reported in Adams County with several trees blown down.  The primary event was the heavy rainfall.  Two to six inches of rain fell over the south half of the state.  The heaviest band extended from the southwest into the central parts of the Des Moines CWA.  Four to Six inches of rain fell from near Creston through Indianola to Oskaloosa resulting in widespread flash flooding.  The highest rainfall report was in the Afton area of Union County with 8.8 inches reported.  Numerous roads and bridges were washed out in the area.  Heavy rainfall across much of south central Iowa left 8 counties still in Areal Flood Warnings on the 10th.  Many roads washed out and some bridges were washed out in Clarke, Ringgold and Madison Counties. Other counties continued with some flooded roads or flooded low water crossings. There was one water rescue in the early morning hours in Madison County that included two separate vehicles at different times. The vehicles hit a low water crossing at 1700 Pitzer Rd and stalled. Both occupants were rescued at the same time by local law enforcement. No injuries were reported with the water rescues. Some rivers went into flood as well with minor and close to moderate flooding occurring within the next few days. Most of the flashy creeks and streams near larger metropolitan areas the CWA were not affected...but smaller streams in some of the more rural counties were the main ones affected.  River flooding occurred in parts of central and southeast Iowa from the 10 through the 12th.  The South, Middle, and North Rivers rose above flood state by one to three feet.  The mainstem Des Moines River at Ottumwa crested about a foot over flood stage.  The Raccoon River also flooded in Des Moines at Fleur Drive with the river cresting about two and one half feet above flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy damage to a machine shed and trees caused by strong straight-line winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful storm system lifted into the central Plains on the afternoon of  April 27, 2014 and brought several rounds of showers and thunderstorms to eastern Iowa. Some of these thunderstorms were severe and produced damaging winds and an isolated hail report. There was one confirmed tornado during the event that moved across Keokuk, Iowa and Johnson Counties with two fatalities. In addition to the damaging winds rainfall totals ranged from a half an inch to around 1 inch in a little under an hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Keokuk County Emergency Manager reported large power poles blown across a road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced numerous events of large hail, damaging winds, flash flooding, and areal flooding across most of northwest Iowa from late morning through the evening of June 16th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage, including limbs up to 6 inches in diameter blown down. The winds were accompanied by small hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong push of very warm air moved into the state on the afternoon of the 6th with high temperatures reaching well into the 90s over the west half of the state.  Dewpoint readings rose to the low 60s.  The atmosphere became unstable, but was capped through much of the day.  By the evening, a cold front began to push into the northwest part of the state. Downdraft CAPE was high, with 1200 to 1700 J/kg available and the freezing level was quite high for early May, around 13,000 feet. A very dynamic weather pattern was in place over over Iowa.  A warm front lifted north into the state.  Temperatures warmed in the 80s with dew point readings in the upper 60s to mid 70s.  The atmosphere became very unstable with 3000 to 4000 J/kg of CAPE and downdraft CAPE of 1000 to 1500 J/kg.  The shear was strong with 50 to 60 kts available.  The freezing level was quite high, between 13,000 and 14,000 feet wtih CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere between 700 and 900 J/kg.  The precipitable water value was a between 1.5 and 1.8 inches.  The LCL was relatively low, around 1000 meters.  Thunderstorms moved into western Iowa out of Nebraska. As the afternoon and evening progressed, the thunderstorm complex evolved into one that was more outflow dominated.  The system raced southeast and produced high winds across the Des Moines metro area and to the east and south.  Winds of 60 to 80 MPH caused considerable tree and power line damage.  Power outages were reported by 27,000 customers in Des Moines at the height of the storm.  As the storms moved through the heart of Des Moines, a homeless individual died when a large tree branch fell on him at a homeless settlement along the banks of the Raccoon River east of Fluer Blvd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated of 75 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms developed late in the afternoon of June 16th across Minnesota and Iowa ahead of warm front and area of low pressure. These storms produced reports of 60 to 70 mph winds as they rolled across northeast Iowa. The area near Westgate (Fayette County) was hit hard with at least three barns blown down, a church roof damaged and numerous trees and power lines were downed. Another cattle barn was blown down south of Brainard (Fayette County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A shed was blown over in Calmar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm system moved across the high Plains during the afternoon on Tuesday June 3rd, and then across the mid Missouri River Valley on Tuesday night. Ahead of the system, warm, moist and unstable air was pulled north as a warm front lifted into eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa by mid afternoon. The combination of the strong vertical wind shear near the boundary, and the strong instability created a volatile atmosphere across the area. The first of several rounds of supercell thunderstorms moved into northeast Nebraska after 1 pm and tracked east southeast. This intense storm moved across northeast Nebraska, and into western and finally southern Iowa producing severe weather. Additional thunderstorms developed ahead and behind this initial wave, also producing severe weather. Additional supercells developed in central Nebraska and moved into southeast Nebraska during the evening hours. The severe weather ended after midnight across the area. Due to the repeated tracking of thunderstorms across the same area, flash flooding was observed in several areas which eventually led to river flooding as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A particularly intense downburst occurred from northwest of Crescent through the town of Cresent to southeast of the city. In this area entire trees were uprooted, outbuildings were completely destroyed, partial roof removal was observed on houses, and crops were stripped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm developed over central Johnson County just before 7 am CDT August 22.  Shortly thereafter it produced damaging wind gusts just east of the I-380 and I-80 interchange in Coralville, IA snapping signs and trees, bending a flag pole, and overturning a semi trailer.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated to be 75 mph overturned a semi trailer at Simpson Furniture (2300 Jones Blvd) and snapped trees and a large billboard sign along Heartland Drive around 705 am CDT August 22.  A residence at 3300 Heartland Drive sustained structural damage when an evergreen tree snapped at the base and fell onto the home.  A nearby flag pole at the home was also bent.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level low pressure was located over the southwest U.S. with surface low pressure located over central Nebraska.  A warm front extended east from the low across Iowa.  Precipitable water values rose to between 1.5 and 2 inches as strong warm air advection and moisture transport took place.  Thunderstorms developed north of the warm front.  Some of the storms became severe with damaging winds and quarter size hail.  Very heavy rain fell, resulting in flash flooding in Kossuth and Humboldt Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs were blown down with parts of the city without power.

Another cattle barn was blown down south of Brainard (Fayette County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down and blocked streets in Osage. One of these trees landed on a car.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Barns were blown down near Westgate with tin strewn across a road. Another barn was blown down with cattle trapped inside the debris, two of which died. Part of a roof was blown off a building used for milking, exposing an office, with the rain soaking office supplies and computers. On the same farm, part of free stall barn was ripped off and a shed was destroyed. Numerous trees and power lines were blown down in town and a church roof sustained damage from the high winds. Some of the trees were 2 to 4 feet in diameter and were either uprooted or snapped off about 20 feet above the ground. Two grain bins were blown over and landed on a building and a truck.

A powerful upper level system was moving into south central Canada.  The upper level winds were increasing as a closed upper low settled into that area.  Warm air advection continued across Iowa through the night of the 29th into the morning of the 30th.  The atmosphere was very unstable with CAPE values around 4000 J/kg.  Downdraft CAPE was between 1000 and 1500 J/kg, with CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere in the 600 to 800 J/kg range.  Strong shear was present, with an effective shear of 50 to 60 kts.  The freezing level was quite high, between 14,000 and 15,000 feet.  Precipitable water increased to between 1.5 and 1.8 inches.  There was a considerable amount of CAPE in the 0-3 km layer of the atmosphere, between 100 and 175 J/kg.  That combined with an LCl of only 750 meters was a favorable environment for tornado development.  Thunderstorms formed rapidly in northwest and north central Iowa by the late afternoon and evening hours.  They spread and continued to move east through the night.  All modes of severe weather occurred with tornadoes occurring during the early and mid evening hours.  Two tornadoes were reported in the Stanhope area near the Webster and Hamilton County line.  A tornado touched down in Grundy County north of Conrad.  Winds caused damage in many areas across the north half of Iowa.  Hail was common with most of the storms in spite of the high freezing level.  Much of the hail an inch or less in diameter.  Larger hail of golf ball to tennis ball size fell in Butler and Bremer Counties.  A strong storm formed in Ringgold and Union Counties and dropped golf ball size hail.  The event produced very heavy rainfall in many locations.  The ground had become nearly saturated from previous rounds of storms during the week prior.  Flooding and flash flooding became a problem in several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were estimated to be gusting to 60 MPH.

Temperatures warmed in the 80s with dew point readings in the upper 60s to mid 70s.  The atmosphere became very unstable with 3000 to 4000 J/kg of CAPE and downdraft CAPE of 1000 to 1500 J/kg.  The shear was strong with 50 to 60 kts available.  The freezing level was quite high, between 13,000 and 14,000 feet wtih CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere between 700 and 900 J/kg.  The precipitable water value was a between 1.5 and 1.8 inches.  The LCL was relatively low, around 1000 meters.  Thunderstorms moved into western Iowa out of Nebraska. As the afternoon and evening progressed, the thunderstorm complex evolved into one that was more outflow dominated.  The system raced southeast and produced high winds across the Des Moines metro area and to the east and south.  Winds of 60 to 80 MPH caused considerable tree and power line damage.  Power outages were reported by 27,000 customers in Des Moines at the height of the storm.  As the storms moved through the heart of Des Moines, a homeless individual died when a large tree branch fell on him at a homeless settlement along the banks of the Raccoon River east of Fluer Blvd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to five inch diameter tree branches down.  Dime size hail and 2 inches of ponding water on road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over Kansas moved northeast during the afternoon of the 11th into the morning of the 12th.  By dawn on the 12th, the low was over northeast Kansas.  A cold frontal boundary extended northeast from the low into northwest Iowa.  The atmosphere became unstable and was strongly sheared.  CAPE had increased to around 3000 J/kg by the late afternoon.  Downdraft CAPE was in the 800 to 1200 J/kg range with available CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere around 600 J/kg.  The lifted index was between -6 and -9 C.  The freezing level had risen to around 14,000 feet.  Deep moisture was drawn into the state on a 40 kt low level jet.  Precipitable water values were in the 1.3 to 1.5 inch range.  The LCL was quite low around 750 meters  The atmosphere was strongly sheared with 55 to 65 kts of effective shear available.  Thunderstorms formed along the cold front with initial development to the southwest of Iowa.  The storms developed northeast into Iowa and became severe.  There were numerous reports of high winds near 70 MPH, along with quarter to ping pong ball sized hail.  Several funnel cloud reports were received as well.  Flash flooding became a problem in Cass County near Atlantic.  A NWS survey team found that 4 tornadoes touched down in Guthrie, Dallas and Boone Counties.  One tornado formed south of Guthrie Center and tracked just over 10 miles northeastward and dissipated northeast of Lake Panorama where high end EF-1 and low EF-2 damage was found.  The average path width was 240 yards with maximum wind speed of 115 MPH.  A second tornado was an EF-1 tornado that formed on the west side of Lake Panorama and tracked east northeast to just south of Yale before dissipating.  The third tornado was just north of Dallas Center in Dallas County and ended about 5 miles northwest of Granger just before reaching HWY 141.  It was an EF-0 tornado with winds of 85 MPH. The fourth tornado was also an EF-0 occurring in Boone County where it touched down to the southeast of Luther.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was snapped.

Downdraft CAPE was in the 800 to 1200 J/kg range with available CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere around 600 J/kg.  The lifted index was between -6 and -9 C.  The freezing level had risen to around 14,000 feet.  Deep moisture was drawn into the state on a 40 kt low level jet.  Precipitable water values were in the 1.3 to 1.5 inch range.  The LCL was quite low around 750 meters  The atmosphere was strongly sheared with 55 to 65 kts of effective shear available.  Thunderstorms formed along the cold front with initial development to the southwest of Iowa.  The storms developed northeast into Iowa and became severe.  There were numerous reports of high winds near 70 MPH, along with quarter to ping pong ball sized hail.  Several funnel cloud reports were received as well.  Flash flooding became a problem in Cass County near Atlantic.  A NWS survey team found that 4 tornadoes touched down in Guthrie, Dallas and Boone Counties.  One tornado formed south of Guthrie Center and tracked just over 10 miles northeastward and dissipated northeast of Lake Panorama where high end EF-1 and low EF-2 damage was found.  The average path width was 240 yards with maximum wind speed of 115 MPH.  A second tornado was an EF-1 tornado that formed on the west side of Lake Panorama and tracked east northeast to just south of Yale before dissipating.  The third tornado was just north of Dallas Center in Dallas County and ended about 5 miles northwest of Granger just before reaching HWY 141.  It was an EF-0 tornado with winds of 85 MPH. The fourth tornado was also an EF-0 occurring in Boone County where it touched down to the southeast of Luther.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few shingles were removed from a new roof.  There was large tree limb damage in town and power lines down east of town. A metal panel was blown off a shed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved southeast across the state during the day on the 26th.  As the low passed to the southeast of the area a strong Arctic cold front moved south across the state.  Temperatures fell from the 40s and 50s into the single digits within a matter of a few hours  Strong northwest winds occurred in the wake of this cold front, along with the sharply falling temperatures.  Sustained winds were in the 30 to 45 MPH range for several hours, with wind gusts to near 65 MPH.  Travel became difficult with reports of some cars and trucks being swept off of the road due to the high cross winds.

A strong low moved from northern Alberta southeast to Lake Superior during the night of the 15th through the day on the 16th. A strong upper level trough was associated with the low. The pressure gradient was strong with winds at the 850 mb level around 50 kts. During the afternoon a pool of very cold air at 850 mb settled into Iowa with temperatures at the core of -24 C. The combination of the atmosphere becoming super-adiabatic during the day and the tightening gradient resulted in strong winds across the state. Winds of 25 to 45 MPH were common with wind gusts over 50 MPH across most of the state. Western Iowa had the highest winds with winds exceeding 58 MPH at several locations. Winds of 61 MPH were reported in Carroll County at Carroll. Atlantic reported a 59 MPH wind gust in Cass County, and a 58 MPH gust was reported in Union County at Creston. Many areas of western Iowa reported sustained winds of 40 MPH. East of the high wind area, a vorticity maximum moved south across central into southeast Iowa. This produced a snow burst with the circulation evident on radar. Thunder accompanied the snow in central Iowa with snowfall of 1 to 3 inches . Visibility was reduced to near zero in heavy snow and blowing snow especially in rural open areas. Visibility increased rapidly as the snow ended as there was no snow on the ground initially, thus blowing snow diminished. Temperatures dropped quickly from the mid 30s into the teens as the snow moved in, allowing for a layer of ice to develop rapidly on roadways. Much of the event hit during the evening commute with several accidents reported with travel becoming hazardous due to the icy conditions and reduced visibilities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very dynamic pattern was in place over the state with a closed low located over southern Canada into the north central U.S.  A very strong jet of 85 to 100 kts as present at 250 mb.  CAPE by early morning was already 3000 to 4000 J/kg with downdraft CAPE of 1000 to 1400 J/kg.  CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was between 1000 and 1400 J/kg.  The freezing level was around 14,000 feet with precipitable water values near 2 inches.  The LCL remained around 750 meters with 75 J/kg of CAPE in the 0-3 km layer.  There was strong shear present, with 60-80 kts of effective shear.  A cold front moved into Iowa during the day and moved across the state.  This event was similar to the event of 29 June 1998.  Thunderstorms formed over northeast Nebraska by sunrise and continued to propagate east into Iowa.  The storms intensified as they moved across the state, into a forward moving MCS.  As it strengthened into a Derecho, winds of 70 to 80 MPH were reported over a large swath of the state.  All modes of severe weather occurred.  Widespread wind damage was reported to trees, power lines, and structures from the high winds.   In addition, very large hail occurred.  Some of the larger hail included three to four inch diameter hail in Calhoun County near Rockwell City, and three and one half inch diameter hail in Warren County at Sandyville.  Several tornadoes were confirmed.    Very heavy rainfall occurred with the storms.  The rain lead to flooding and flash flooding in many areas of northern and central Iowa.  By the end of the month, a presidential disaster declaration had been made for the follow 9 Iowa counties: Adams, Clarke, Decatur, Mills, Montgomery, Pottawattamie, Ringgold, Taylor and Wayne.  Governor Branstad also requested a disaster declaration for the following 26 Iowa Counties: Allamakee, Buchanan, Buena Vista, Butler, Cherokee, Chickasaw, Clay, Dickinson, Emmet, Fayette, Franklin, Hancock, Humboldt, Ida, Kossuth, Lyon, Osceola, Palo Alto, Plymouth, Pocahontas, Sac, Sioux, Winnebago, Winneshiek, Woodbury, and Wright.  The Presidential declaration was granted on 24 July 2014.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured by home weather station.  Two large ash trees snapped.  Pine tree snapped with corn and soy beans flattened.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of the 7th, a boundary extended west-northwest to east-southeast across southwest Iowa.  The atmosphere became unstable with CAPE of 3000 to 4000 J/kg by late afternoon.  Downdraft CAPE was around 900 J/kg, with 300 to 600 J/kg of CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere.  The shear was stronger than the day before, with about 50 kts of effective shear . CAPE in the 0-3 km layer was 25 to 75 J/kg.  The lifted index had fallen to -8 C.  Precipitable water increased to 1.6 to 1.8 inches.  The freezing level was quite high, between 14,000 and 14,500 feet.  Low level lapse rates were between 6 and 7 C with the LCL fairly high at 1250 meters.  Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon in Nebraska where the cap weakened first.  They spread into southern Iowa by the evening hours.  As the line of storm intensified, a MARC signature was evident on the Radar.  Winds began to reach severe levels as the storms moved through the Creston area.  The strongest wind gusts were in the Lamoni area, where a 74 MPH wind gust was recorded.  Significant tree and power line damage was done across the city.  Governor Terry E. Branstad signed a letter to be delivered to President Obama requesting a Presidential Disaster Declaration for 22 Iowa counties where significant damage was sustained from severe weather that produced damaging winds, tornadoes, heavy rains, hail, and thunderstorms beginning June 26 through July 7, 2014.  The counties included in the request are: Audubon, Black Hawk, Butler, Cedar, Des Moines, Grundy, Hamilton, Hardin, Ida, Iowa, Jackson, Jasper, Johnson, Jones, Keokuk, Lee, Linn, Mahaska, Muscatine, Poweshiek, Tama and Washington.  President Obama has approved Governor Branstad���s request for a Presidential Disaster Declaration for 22 counties impacted by severe weather during the period of June 26-July 7.  The declaration will provide funding under the Public Assistance and Hazard Mitigation Programs.

Severe thunderstorms developed during the evening of June 29th ahead of a cold front. As these storms moved across northeast Iowa they produced large hail and damaging winds. Two pole sheds were destroyed near Boyd (Chickasaw County) where baseball sized hail also fell. The large hail destroyed crops in an area from Nashua northeast toward Boyd. In Calmar (Winneshiek County), several trees were blown down and golf ball sized hail damaged several vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down in Nashua.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated wind gust of 60 mph occurred in Waukon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance moving across the region caused a warm front to lift northward across eastern Iowa during the late afternoon and evening. This led to the development of severe thunderstorms across eastern Iowa north of Interstate 80. These thunderstorms produced strong wind gusts over 60 mph and torrential rains with 1 to 4 inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A person from the general public reported uprooted trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The weather pattern changed only slightly from the day before.  A strong upper level trough was in place to the west of Iowa with the surface low and cold front near the western Iowa border, and the warm front bisecting the state.  The airmass was very moist with surface dew points around 70 F and precipitable water values in the 1.5 to 2 inch range.  Severe weather was somewhat limited.  Some sub-severe hail was reported, with scattered reports of damaging winds.  Heavy rainfall of several inches in places resulted in significant flash flooding across northern Iowa.  Statewide flood damage for Iowa was assessed shortly after the rainfall and was estimated at $15.5 million for public buildings, bridges, infrastructure and roads.  Note that this estimate does not include crop damage as well as damage to private businesses and up to 150 homes across the state.  Governor Brandstad issued disaster declarations for the following 18 counties:  Buena Vista, Cedar, Cherokee, Clay, Dickinson, Franklin, Hancock, Ida, Kossuth, Lyon, Osceola, Palo Alto, Plymouth, Pocahontas, Sac, Sioux, Woodbury and Wright.  Iowa Homeland Security planed to seek a Presidential disaster declaration.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Monroe County sheriff reported traffic sign damage and a metal shed roof removed with the shed leaning near 145th Trail and Highway 5.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of the 7th, a boundary extended west-northwest to east-southeast across southwest Iowa.  The atmosphere became unstable with CAPE of 3000 to 4000 J/kg by late afternoon.  Downdraft CAPE was around 900 J/kg, with 300 to 600 J/kg of CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere.  The shear was stronger than the day before, with about 50 kts of effective shear . CAPE in the 0-3 km layer was 25 to 75 J/kg.  The lifted index had fallen to -8 C.  Precipitable water increased to 1.6 to 1.8 inches.  The freezing level was quite high, between 14,000 and 14,500 feet.  Low level lapse rates were between 6 and 7 C with the LCL fairly high at 1250 meters.  Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon in Nebraska where the cap weakened first.  They spread into southern Iowa by the evening hours.  As the line of storm intensified, a MARC signature was evident on the Radar.  Winds began to reach severe levels as the storms moved through the Creston area.  The strongest wind gusts were in the Lamoni area, where a 74 MPH wind gust was recorded.  Significant tree and power line damage was done across the city.  Governor Terry E. Branstad signed a letter to be delivered to President Obama requesting a Presidential Disaster Declaration for 22 Iowa counties where significant damage was sustained from severe weather that produced damaging winds, tornadoes, heavy rains, hail, and thunderstorms beginning June 26 through July 7, 2014.  The counties included in the request are: Audubon, Black Hawk, Butler, Cedar, Des Moines, Grundy, Hamilton, Hardin, Ida, Iowa, Jackson, Jasper, Johnson, Jones, Keokuk, Lee, Linn, Mahaska, Muscatine, Poweshiek, Tama and Washington. The declaration will provide funding under the Public Assistance and Hazard Mitigation Programs.

Severe thunderstorms developed during the evening of June 29th ahead of a cold front. As these storms moved across northeast Iowa they produced large hail and damaging winds. Two pole sheds were destroyed near Boyd (Chickasaw County) where baseball sized hail also fell. The large hail destroyed crops in an area from Nashua northeast toward Boyd. In Calmar (Winneshiek County), several trees were blown down and golf ball sized hail damaged several vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were either blown down or uprooted on and near a golf course east of New Hampton. One of the downed trees caused one lane of State Highway 24 to be closed, a semi trailer was blown over onto its side and an outbuilding was destroyed. The cart shelter at the golf course was also damaged by the high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very dynamic weather pattern was in place over over Iowa.  A warm front lifted north into the state.  Temperatures warmed in the 80s with dew point readings in the upper 60s to mid 70s.  The atmosphere became very unstable with 3000 to 4000 J/kg of CAPE and downdraft CAPE of 1000 to 1500 J/kg.  The shear was strong with 50 to 60 kts available.  The freezing level was quite high, between 13,000 and 14,000 feet wtih CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere between 700 and 900 J/kg.  The precipitable water value was a between 1.5 and 1.8 inches.  The LCL was relatively low, around 1000 meters.  Thunderstorms moved into western Iowa out of Nebraska. As the afternoon and evening progressed, the thunderstorm complex evolved into one that was more outflow dominated.  The system raced southeast and produced high winds across the Des Moines metro area and to the east and south.  Winds of 60 to 80 MPH caused considerable tree and power line damage.  Power outages were reported by 27,000 customers in Des Moines at the height of the storm.  As the storms moved through the heart of Des Moines, a homeless individual died when a large tree branch fell on him at a homeless settlement along the banks of the Raccoon River east of Fluer Blvd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Narrow but strong area of wind damage in east central Bremer County. Worst damage was on a farmstead (point used for this event). A barn was flattened, multiple trees were snapped, and several empty grain bins were thrown into the trees.

A cold front moved through the state during the afternoon of the 6th.  Temperatures warmed into the mid 80s to around 90, with dew points in the mid 70s to low 80s.  The atmosphere became very unstable with CAPE rising to 8000 J/kg by late afternoon.  Downdraft CAPE was around 1200 J/kg, with CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere between 800 and 1100 J/kg.  The freezing level was high, around 15,000 feet with a 700 mb temperature around 12 C.  Significant capping was in place.  Precipitable water was between 1.6 and 1.8 inches with an LCL around 1000 meters.  The lifted index fell to -13 C.  There was a significant amount of CAPE in the 0-3 km layer of the atmosphere along the front, between 150 and 175 J/kg.  Shear was quite weak however, with only 25 kts of effective shear available.  The non-severe storm parameter was around 0.5 over northeast Iowa near the cold front.  Eight tornadoes occurred during the evening.  Fortunately, the tornadoes missed cities, though they did do considerable crop damage.  There was little other severe weather reported with the storms.  One inch diameter hail fell in Poweshiek County, while winds gusted to 60 MPH or more in Wapello County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported a power pole downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very dynamic pattern was in place over the state with a closed low located over southern Canada into the north central U.S.  A very strong jet of 85 to 100 kts as present at 250 mb.  CAPE by early morning was already 3000 to 4000 J/kg with downdraft CAPE of 1000 to 1400 J/kg.  CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was between 1000 and 1400 J/kg.  The freezing level was around 14,000 feet with precipitable water values near 2 inches.  The LCL remained around 750 meters with 75 J/kg of CAPE in the 0-3 km layer.  There was strong shear present, with 60-80 kts of effective shear.  A cold front moved into Iowa during the day and moved across the state.  This event was similar to the event of 29 June 1998.  Thunderstorms formed over northeast Nebraska by sunrise and continued to propagate east into Iowa.  The storms intensified as they moved across the state, into a forward moving MCS.  As it strengthened into a Derecho, winds of 70 to 80 MPH were reported over a large swath of the state.  All modes of severe weather occurred.  Widespread wind damage was reported to trees, power lines, and structures from the high winds.   In addition, very large hail occurred.  Some of the larger hail included three to four inch diameter hail in Calhoun County near Rockwell City, and three and one half inch diameter hail in Warren County at Sandyville.  Several tornadoes were confirmed.    Very heavy rainfall occurred with the storms.  The rain lead to flooding and flash flooding in many areas of northern and central Iowa.  By the end of the month, a presidential disaster declaration had been made for the follow 9 Iowa counties: Adams, Clarke, Decatur, Mills, Montgomery, Pottawattamie, Ringgold, Taylor and Wayne.  Governor Branstad also requested a disaster declaration for the following 26 Iowa Counties: Allamakee, Buchanan, Buena Vista, Butler, Cherokee, Chickasaw, Clay, Dickinson, Emmet, Fayette, Franklin, Hancock, Humboldt, Ida, Kossuth, Lyon, Osceola, Palo Alto, Plymouth, Pocahontas, Sac, Sioux, Winnebago, Winneshiek, Woodbury, and Wright.  The Presidential declaration was granted on 24 July 2014.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree branches broken off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front during the afternoon of June 1st. Moderate instability and modest shear ahead of the boundary allowed this convection to develop across eastern Nebraska by mid afternoon. This evolved into a line of storms which continued east into western Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public estimated the gust in Missouri Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front extended from eastern Nebraska to western Illinois and was lifting northward on June 16-17, 2014.  An extremely unstable air mass was over the area on June 16 along with strong deep layer shear.  This allowed scattered supercells to form during the afternoon and evening which produced two tornadoes, very large hail and damaging winds.  A weak tornado formed 4 miles south of Lamont, Iowa in Buchanan County, tracked about 1 mile to the northeast before crossing into Delaware County. In Delaware County the tornado continued for about 13 miles to the northeast to Edgewood, Iowa.  Another weak tornado briefly touched down in Benton County, Iowa. Strengthening mid level winds along with a strong low level jet in place later in the evening and overnight on June 17 produced widespread damaging winds of 60 to 70 mph.  Strong warm air advection triggered more severe thunderstorms during the late evening of June 17.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree branches of 15 inches in diameter blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed into a very warm unstable air mass as a significant upper level storm system tracked into the northern Great Lakes on June 29-30, 2014. A major severe weather and flash flood event unfolded across the area. This event resulted in the direct deaths of 2 people and direct injuries to 7 others.  Three weak tornadoes, widespread damaging winds of 60 to 80 mph, and life-threatening flash flooding due to 4 to 6 inches of rainfall was reported. Two of the tornadoes were in northeast Iowa while another tornado was spotted in northwest Illinois.  Law enforcement reported a business building destroyed by high winds from a severe thunderstorm in Fairfax, Iowa. One person was killed and another injured due to the collapsed structure.  A severe thunderstorm packing 80 mph winds blew a tarp on top of several people trying to cover the infield at Veterans Memorial Stadium in Cedar Rapids, Iowa. Three people suffered concussions while another person had a dislocated shoulder.  In North Liberty, Iowa a tree fell on a person who was then transported to a local hospital.  Severe flash flooding in Cedar Rapids, Iowa resulted in the drowning death of a teenage boy with another boy injured.  They were swept into a storm sewer and were found a mile away in a lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter estimated 60 mph winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were measured at 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of thunderstorms formed over northeast Nebraska in an areas of strong theta-e advection.  Precipitable water values were only around 1.3 inches.  CAPE was in the 1000 to 2000 J/kg range with a downdraft CAPE of 800 J/kg.  The atmosphere was strongly sheared with about 50 kts of effective shear present.  The area of storms raced east at 55 to 65 MPH.  The storm produced high winds, as well as tree and power line damage, as it moved through Kossuth, Humboldt, and Wright Counties.  Wright County was hardest hit with some structural damage done to homes west of Clarion.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous three to four inch diameter tree limbs were downed on Highway 169.

Two of the tornadoes were in northeast Iowa while another tornado was spotted in northwest Illinois.  Law enforcement reported a business building destroyed by high winds from a severe thunderstorm in Fairfax, Iowa. One person was killed and another injured due to the collapsed structure.  A severe thunderstorm packing 80 mph winds blew a tarp on top of several people trying to cover the infield at Veterans Memorial Stadium in Cedar Rapids, Iowa. Three people suffered concussions while another person had a dislocated shoulder.  In North Liberty, Iowa a tree fell on a person who was then transported to a local hospital.  Severe flash flooding in Cedar Rapids, Iowa resulted in the drowning death of a teenage boy with another boy injured.  They were swept into a storm sewer and were found a mile away in a lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The fire department estimated winds of 60 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The top half of a barn was blown off and a house was severely damaged.  At the house, the front porch blew off and the garage doors blew in, unroofing the garage and front half of the roof of the house.  Radar showed a strong downburst occurring at the time of the damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system lifted northeast out of the southeast U.S. during the day on the 8th.  Surface low pressure was located over northwest Kansas in the morning and tracked northeast into southern Minnesota by the evening.  Moisture was drawn north ahead of the low with precipitable water values rising to around 1.3 inches.  Surface dewpoints rose into the low to mid 60s.  The atmosphere was unstable with the lifted index falling to -6 C and CAPE values around 2000 J/kg.  Downdraft CAPE was in the 800 to 1200 J/kg range.  The flow was around 40 kts at 850 mb from the south-southwest.  The atmosphere was moderately sheared with 30 to 40 kts of effective shear.  The LCL was relatively high around 1250 meters.  CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was around 400 J/kg with the freezing level only 12,000 feet.  Thunderstorms developed over west central into northwest Iowa along a cold front that pushed into the state.  The storms produced winds of up to 70 MPH in Kossuth County near Algona.  Another storm produced quarter size hail in Carroll County west of Willey.  A severe thunderstorm developed over south central Iowa southwest of the Des Moines metro area.  This storm produced large hail with a swath of hail extending form Madison County through the Des Moines area before it weakened.  There were numerous reports of golf ball size hail.  Baseball size hail fell on the northwest side of the Metro east of Urbandale.  The storm also produced gusty winds in the 55 to 60 mph range reported at a few locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter estimated 60 MPH wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture returning to eastern Nebraska and western Iowa during the afternoon of June 20th allowed for the development of scattered thunderstorms. The thunderstorms were responsible for mainly large hail across the area, but some strong winds were reported as well. The thunderstorms also dropped very heavy rainfall, especially in the Omaha metro area, which led to significant flash flooding in parts of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The local fire department estimated wind gusts to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front extended from eastern Nebraska to western Illinois and was lifting northward on June 16-17, 2014.  An extremely unstable air mass was over the area on June 16 along with strong deep layer shear.  This allowed scattered supercells to form during the afternoon and evening which produced two tornadoes, very large hail and damaging winds.  A weak tornado formed 4 miles south of Lamont, Iowa in Buchanan County, tracked about 1 mile to the northeast before crossing into Delaware County. In Delaware County the tornado continued for about 13 miles to the northeast to Edgewood, Iowa.  Another weak tornado briefly touched down in Benton County, Iowa. Strengthening mid level winds along with a strong low level jet in place later in the evening and overnight on June 17 produced widespread damaging winds of 60 to 70 mph.  Strong warm air advection triggered more severe thunderstorms during the late evening of June 17.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were snapped and tree limbs blown down in and around the southwest corner of Scott county park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of thunderstorms formed over northeast Nebraska in an areas of strong theta-e advection.  Precipitable water values were only around 1.3 inches.  CAPE was in the 1000 to 2000 J/kg range with a downdraft CAPE of 800 J/kg.  The atmosphere was strongly sheared with about 50 kts of effective shear present.  The area of storms raced east at 55 to 65 MPH.  The storm produced high winds, as well as tree and power line damage, as it moved through Kossuth, Humboldt, and Wright Counties.  Wright County was hardest hit with some structural damage done to homes west of Clarion.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous power lines down causing a 3 hour power outage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A seasonably strong upper level storm system swept across the northern Plains during the day on Sunday August 31st. Ahead of the storm system low pressure developed over the high Plains and allowed for warm and moist air to pool over the mid Missouri River Valley creating strong instability by mid to late afternoon. As a cold front and associated upper level jet streak interacted with the instability, thunderstorms developed from northeast Nebraska into Northern Kansas. These thunderstorms moved east through the rest of the afternoon and evening hours, crossing eastern Nebraska and western Iowa produced mainly hail and wind damage, but heavy rainfall also occurred in parts of the area as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The gust was estimated by the a trained spotter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved southeast across the state during the day on the 26th.  As the low passed to the southeast of the area a strong Arctic cold front moved south across the state.  Temperatures fell from the 40s and 50s into the single digits within a matter of a few hours  Strong northwest winds occurred in the wake of this cold front, along with the sharply falling temperatures.  Sustained winds were in the 30 to 45 MPH range for several hours, with wind gusts to near 65 MPH.  Travel became difficult with reports of some cars and trucks being swept off of the road due to the high cross winds.

An upper level disturbance moved across eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois August 25, sparking some scattered showers and thunderstorms over areas north of Interstate 80.  Some of the thunderstorms were severe, producing damaging winds from Clinton County in Iowa to Whiteside and Henry Counties in Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated to be 70 mph blew a tree down onto a house in Pleasant Valley, IA around 317 pm CDT August 25.  The gusty winds also caused some roof damage to the home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very dynamic weather pattern was in place over over Iowa.  A warm front lifted north into the state.  Temperatures warmed in the 80s with dew point readings in the upper 60s to mid 70s.  The atmosphere became very unstable with 3000 to 4000 J/kg of CAPE and downdraft CAPE of 1000 to 1500 J/kg.  The shear was strong with 50 to 60 kts available.  The freezing level was quite high, between 13,000 and 14,000 feet wtih CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere between 700 and 900 J/kg.  The precipitable water value was a between 1.5 and 1.8 inches.  The LCL was relatively low, around 1000 meters.  Thunderstorms moved into western Iowa out of Nebraska. As the afternoon and evening progressed, the thunderstorm complex evolved into one that was more outflow dominated.  The system raced southeast and produced high winds across the Des Moines metro area and to the east and south.  Winds of 60 to 80 MPH caused considerable tree and power line damage.  Power outages were reported by 27,000 customers in Des Moines at the height of the storm.  As the storms moved through the heart of Des Moines, a homeless individual died when a large tree branch fell on him at a homeless settlement along the banks of the Raccoon River east of Fluer Blvd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two empty grain bins ripped from foundation and thrown 75 yards to the SE. Swath of area 4 miles south and several miles east of this point also had tree limb damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very dynamic pattern was in place over the state with a closed low located over southern Canada into the north central U.S.  A very strong jet of 85 to 100 kts as present at 250 mb.  CAPE by early morning was already 3000 to 4000 J/kg with downdraft CAPE of 1000 to 1400 J/kg.  CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was between 1000 and 1400 J/kg.  The freezing level was around 14,000 feet with precipitable water values near 2 inches.  The LCL remained around 750 meters with 75 J/kg of CAPE in the 0-3 km layer.  There was strong shear present, with 60-80 kts of effective shear.  A cold front moved into Iowa during the day and moved across the state.  This event was similar to the event of 29 June 1998.  Thunderstorms formed over northeast Nebraska by sunrise and continued to propagate east into Iowa.  The storms intensified as they moved across the state, into a forward moving MCS.  As it strengthened into a Derecho, winds of 70 to 80 MPH were reported over a large swath of the state.  All modes of severe weather occurred.  Widespread wind damage was reported to trees, power lines, and structures from the high winds.   In addition, very large hail occurred.  Some of the larger hail included three to four inch diameter hail in Calhoun County near Rockwell City, and three and one half inch diameter hail in Warren County at Sandyville.  Several tornadoes were confirmed.    Very heavy rainfall occurred with the storms.  The rain lead to flooding and flash flooding in many areas of northern and central Iowa.  By the end of the month, a presidential disaster declaration had been made for the follow 9 Iowa counties: Adams, Clarke, Decatur, Mills, Montgomery, Pottawattamie, Ringgold, Taylor and Wayne.  Governor Branstad also requested a disaster declaration for the following 26 Iowa Counties: Allamakee, Buchanan, Buena Vista, Butler, Cherokee, Chickasaw, Clay, Dickinson, Emmet, Fayette, Franklin, Hancock, Humboldt, Ida, Kossuth, Lyon, Osceola, Palo Alto, Plymouth, Pocahontas, Sac, Sioux, Winnebago, Winneshiek, Woodbury, and Wright.  The Presidential declaration was granted on 24 July 2014.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple tree limbs down by winds estimated between 60 and 70 MPH.

Governor Branstad also requested a disaster declaration for the following 26 Iowa Counties: Allamakee, Buchanan, Buena Vista, Butler, Cherokee, Chickasaw, Clay, Dickinson, Emmet, Fayette, Franklin, Hancock, Humboldt, Ida, Kossuth, Lyon, Osceola, Palo Alto, Plymouth, Pocahontas, Sac, Sioux, Winnebago, Winneshiek, Woodbury, and Wright.  The Presidential declaration was granted on 24 July 2014.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous 40 to 50 foot trees down in Zearing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed into a very warm unstable air mass as a significant upper level storm system tracked into the northern Great Lakes on June 29-30, 2014. A major severe weather and flash flood event unfolded across the area. This event resulted in the direct deaths of 2 people and direct injuries to 7 others.  Three weak tornadoes, widespread damaging winds of 60 to 80 mph, and life-threatening flash flooding due to 4 to 6 inches of rainfall was reported. Two of the tornadoes were in northeast Iowa while another tornado was spotted in northwest Illinois.  Law enforcement reported a business building destroyed by high winds from a severe thunderstorm in Fairfax, Iowa. One person was killed and another injured due to the collapsed structure.  A severe thunderstorm packing 80 mph winds blew a tarp on top of several people trying to cover the infield at Veterans Memorial Stadium in Cedar Rapids, Iowa. Three people suffered concussions while another person had a dislocated shoulder.  In North Liberty, Iowa a tree fell on a person who was then transported to a local hospital.  Severe flash flooding in Cedar Rapids, Iowa resulted in the drowning death of a teenage boy with another boy injured.  They were swept into a storm sewer and were found a mile away in a lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree branches down heading toward Alburnett.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The atmosphere remained unstable with a continued influx of warm and moist air into the state.  Thunderstorms were fairly widespread, but most only produced very heavy rainfall, small hail, and gusty winds.  A few storms in Marshall and Tama County produced sever winds with tree branches broken northwest of Toledo in Tama County and a dugout at a school destroyed by high winds south of Marshalltown in Marshall County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A dugout at a school was destroyed by high winds.  Power was out through much of the town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved slowly south into the state during the day on the 20th.  The flow was quite light with overall weak dynamics associated with the system.  The atmosphere became unstable by afternoon with around 2000 J/kg of CAPE available.  The downdraft CAPE was quite high in the 900 to 1200 J/kg range.  CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was between 300 and 600 J/kg.  Precipitable water was around 1.3 inches, with a freezing level in the 13,000 to 13,500 foot level.  Thunderstorms developed rapidly along and near the 850 mb front extending from north of Omaha to near Dubuque.  They initially produced hail with penny size hail reported in Audubon County.  The storms formed into a scattered line of severe thunderstorms and produced hail up to golf ball size.  Ames received golf ball size hail across much of the city.  As the storms continued one of the cells began producing high winds.  Winds of up to 65 MPH occurred in Story County around Huxley and Maxwell.  The high winds damaged barns and outbuildings at several locations.  Heavy rainfall of 2 to 3 inches fell over Story County, resulting in flash flooding, especially in the Maxwell area.  Most of the rain fell in a one to two hour time period.  Many ditches and farm fields were flooded.  Highway 210 was under water near Maxwell for a period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gust was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front extended from eastern Nebraska to western Illinois and was lifting northward on June 16-17, 2014.  An extremely unstable air mass was over the area on June 16 along with strong deep layer shear.  This allowed scattered supercells to form during the afternoon and evening which produced two tornadoes, very large hail and damaging winds.  A weak tornado formed 4 miles south of Lamont, Iowa in Buchanan County, tracked about 1 mile to the northeast before crossing into Delaware County. In Delaware County the tornado continued for about 13 miles to the northeast to Edgewood, Iowa.  Another weak tornado briefly touched down in Benton County, Iowa. Strengthening mid level winds along with a strong low level jet in place later in the evening and overnight on June 17 produced widespread damaging winds of 60 to 70 mph.  Strong warm air advection triggered more severe thunderstorms during the late evening of June 17.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small tree branches down due to 60 mph winds.
